description: Additional practice exercises for the ACP course. NOT the 10 mandatory lab programs (see lab_programs.json). Exercises are categorised as: 'prerequisite' (foundation, assumed from prior course), 'syllabus' (ACP CO-aligned practice), or 'advanced' (beyond the syllabus, for fast finishers).
department: School of Computer Science and Engineering
course: Advanced C Programming with Generative AI

co_descriptions.CO1: Develop modular and reusable code using functions (call by value and call by reference), arrays and structures.
co_descriptions.CO2: Apply modular programming to manage complex data collections using Arrays of Structures.
co_descriptions.CO3: Develop modular and reusable code using pointers and dynamic memory allocation.
co_descriptions.CO4: Develop modular and reusable code utilizing Strings by implementing user-defined logic for processing text-based data.
co_descriptions.CO5: Develop a modular application for file handling in C.
co_descriptions.CO6: Leverage generative AI to design and build a mini-project application.

INTRO.name: Introduction to C
INTRO.description: Program structure, printf, scanf, compilation. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Hello, World
exercises.concept_under_test: printf, program structure, compilation
exercises.problem_statement: Write a C program that prints 'Hello, World!' followed by a newline.
constraints: Use printf
main must return int
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what #include <stdio.h> does
Can state what printf returns

common_mistakes.mistake: Missing newline in printf
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does your output look like if the shell prompt appears on the same line as your output?

exercises.grading_notes: Simplest possible exercise — full marks requires correct output AND correct main signature.

exercises.title: Fahrenheit to Celsius converter
exercises.concept_under_test: arithmetic expressions, float I/O, type casting
exercises.problem_statement: Read a temperature in Fahrenheit (floating-point). Convert and print it in Celsius to 2 decimal places. Formula: C = (F - 32) * 5 / 9
constraints: Use float or double
Use %.2f format specifier
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain integer division pitfall: 5/9 = 0 in C
Can state difference between 5/9 and 5.0/9

common_mistakes.mistake: Integer division 5/9 gives 0
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does 5/9 evaluate to in C when both operands are int? How could you force floating-point division?

style_requirements: Meaningful variable names (not just f and c)
exercises.grading_notes: Integer division is the key teaching point.

DTYPES.name: Data Types and Variables
DTYPES.description: Primitive types, sizeof, limits. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Integer overflow demonstration
exercises.concept_under_test: int size, overflow, limits.h
exercises.problem_statement: Print the maximum value of int on this system using INT_MAX from <limits.h>. Then print INT_MAX + 1 and observe overflow.
constraints: Include <limits.h>
Use int, not long long
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why adding 1 to INT_MAX wraps to a negative number
Can state the width of int in bits on a 32-bit system

common_mistakes.mistake: Hardcoding the value instead of using INT_MAX
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What would happen to your program if it ran on a 16-bit system where int is 16 bits? Would your hardcoded value still be correct?

exercises.grading_notes: The goal is understanding overflow, not just printing.

OPS.name: Operators and Expressions
OPS.description: Arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise operators. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Bitwise operations on two integers
exercises.concept_under_test: bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, left/right shift
exercises.problem_statement: Read two non-negative integers A and B. Print: A AND B, A OR B, A XOR B, NOT A (bitwise), A left-shifted by 1, B right-shifted by 1.
constraints: Use int
Print each result on its own line with the label shown
mental_model_checkpoints: Can convert 12 and 10 to binary and trace AND manually
Can explain why ~12 gives -13 (two's complement)

common_mistakes.mistake: Confusing & (bitwise) with && (logical)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the difference between & and && in C? What does each one return?

IO.name: Input and Output
IO.description: scanf/printf format specifiers. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Read and echo three values of different types
exercises.concept_under_test: scanf format specifiers, printf format specifiers, mixed types
exercises.problem_statement: Read an int, a float, and a char (in that order, space-separated). Print them back on one line in the same order, with the float to 2 decimal places.
constraints: One scanf call per variable
Use %d, %f, %c appropriately
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what & does in scanf(&n)
Can explain why %f reads float but %lf reads double

common_mistakes.mistake: Using %d for float or %f for int
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does the format specifier tell scanf? What happens if the specifier doesn't match the variable's type?

COND.name: Conditional Statements
COND.description: if-else, switch. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Grade letter from percentage
exercises.concept_under_test: if-else if-else chain, comparison operators
exercises.problem_statement: Read an integer percentage (0–100). Print the letter grade: A (>=90), B (>=80), C (>=70), D (>=60), F (<60).
constraints: Use if-else if-else (not switch)
Do not use lookup tables
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what happens when percentage=90 reaches the first condition
Can explain why order of conditions matters

common_mistakes.mistake: Reversed condition order causes wrong grade for boundary values
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Trace your code for input 90. Which branch does it enter first? Is that the correct one?

exercises.title: Simple calculator with switch
exercises.concept_under_test: switch statement, fall-through, char input, division by zero
exercises.problem_statement: Read two doubles A and B, then a char operator (+, -, *, /). Print the result to 2 decimal places. If operator is '/' and B is 0, print 'Error: division by zero'.
constraints: Use switch for the operator
Handle division by zero explicitly
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what happens if you forget a break in switch
Can explain how to check for 0.0 with floating-point

common_mistakes.mistake: Missing break causes fall-through to next case
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What happens in your switch when the operator is '+' and you're missing the break after the '+' case? Walk through the execution.

LOOP.name: Loops
LOOP.description: for, while, do-while loops. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Print first N natural numbers
exercises.concept_under_test: for loop, initialization, condition, increment
exercises.problem_statement: Read a positive integer N. Print all natural numbers from 1 to N, one per line.
constraints: Use a for loop
Use scanf for input
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state value of loop variable after each iteration
Can identify loop termination condition in plain English

common_mistakes.mistake: Loop starts from 0 instead of 1
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the very first value your loop variable i has when the loop body first runs?

common_mistakes.mistake: Off-by-one: loop runs to N-1
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: For N=4, how many times does your loop body execute? Count them.

exercises.title: Sum of first N natural numbers
exercises.concept_under_test: for loop, accumulator pattern
exercises.problem_statement: Read a positive integer N. Compute and print the sum 1 + 2 + ... + N.
constraints: Use a for loop
Use an accumulator variable (not the formula n*(n+1)/2)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state the value of sum after each iteration for N=3
Can explain why the accumulator must be initialised to 0 before the loop

common_mistakes.mistake: Accumulator not initialised to 0
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What value does a local int variable have in C before you assign it? Is that a safe assumption for a sum accumulator?

exercises.title: Count the digits of a number
exercises.concept_under_test: while loop, integer division as loop engine
exercises.problem_statement: Read a positive integer N. Print the number of digits it has.
constraints: Use a while loop
Use integer division (no string conversion, no log10)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what N/10 does to the number each iteration
Can explain the loop termination condition

common_mistakes.mistake: Loop condition is N > 0 but counter starts at 0 — misses one digit
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Trace your loop for N=7. How many times does the loop body run? Should it run once or zero times?

exercises.title: Sum of digits of an integer
exercises.concept_under_test: while loop, modulo operator, handling negative numbers
exercises.problem_statement: Read an integer N (may be negative). Compute and print the sum of its digits. For N = -123, the answer is 6.
constraints: Handle negative numbers
Use a while loop
Do not convert to string
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what N % 10 gives for N = 123
Can explain what N / 10 does to the number each iteration
Can handle N = 0 as an edge case

common_mistakes.mistake: Fails for N=0 (loop never executes)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the value of N before your while loop starts when input is 0? Does your condition allow the loop to run even once?

common_mistakes.mistake: Fails for negative N (modulo is negative in C)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does -123 % 10 evaluate to in C? Is that what you expected for digit extraction?

exercises.grading_notes: Negative modulo behaviour is the key teaching point.

exercises.title: Check if a number is prime
exercises.concept_under_test: while/for loop, trial division, early exit with break
exercises.problem_statement: Read a positive integer N (N >= 2). Print 'Prime' if it is prime, 'Not prime' otherwise.
constraints: Only check divisors up to sqrt(N)
Use a flag variable or early break
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why checking up to sqrt(N) is sufficient
Can trace what happens for N=4: does 2 divide 4?

common_mistakes.mistake: Loops from 2 to N-1 (inefficient but correct)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If you find that 4 divides 16, do you need to keep checking 5, 6, 7? What is the highest divisor you ever need to check?

common_mistakes.mistake: Marks 2 as not prime
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Trace your code for N=2. What does your loop do — does it enter at all? What does your flag start as?

exercises.grading_notes: Efficiency: trial division up to sqrt(N) is required for full marks.

exercises.title: Pascal's Triangle (first N rows)
exercises.concept_under_test: nested loops, 2D array accumulation, combinatorial thinking
exercises.problem_statement: Read N (N <= 15). Print the first N rows of Pascal's Triangle. Each row's values are separated by a single space.
constraints: Store triangle in a 2D array
No factorial function
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state the relationship between triangle[i][j] and the two elements above it
Can identify the boundary conditions for first and last element in each row

common_mistakes.mistake: Array not initialised — garbage values appear
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the value of triangle[2][1] after your array declaration but before your loops run?

exercises.grading_notes: 2D array initialisation to 0 is required. Boundary conditions for first/last element per row must be explicit.

JUMP.name: Jump Statements
JUMP.description: break, continue, goto. Assumed known from prior course.

exercises.title: Print numbers skipping multiples of 3
exercises.concept_under_test: continue statement
exercises.problem_statement: Read N. Print all integers from 1 to N, skipping any that are divisible by 3. Use the continue statement.
constraints: Must use continue (not a conditional printf)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what continue does versus break
Can trace loop for N=6 step by step

common_mistakes.mistake: Using break instead of continue
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does break do when the loop variable equals 3? What does continue do? Which one do you want here?

FUNC.name: Functions
FUNC.description: ACP Unit 1 — Function definition, prototypes, call by value, call by reference, scope. Additional practice beyond the lab programs.

exercises.title: Factorial using a function (iterative)
exercises.concept_under_test: function definition, return value, function call, call by value
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function long long factorial(int n) that returns n! (0 <= n <= 20) using a loop. Call it from main for a user-supplied n.
constraints: No recursion for this exercise
Use a loop inside the function
Return type must be long long
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state where the function's return value goes when called
Can trace the function execution for n=0

common_mistakes.mistake: Returns int instead of long long — overflow for n >= 13
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is 13! as a number? What is the maximum value an int can hold on a 32-bit system?

exercises.grading_notes: Return type is a common mistake. 0! = 1 edge case must be handled.

exercises.title: Power function (integer exponent)
exercises.concept_under_test: function definition, return value, loop inside function, call by value
function_prototypes: long long power(int base, int exp);
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function long long power(int base, int exp) that returns base^exp for exp >= 0. Do not use pow() from math.h.
constraints: Do not use pow() from math.h
Handle exp=0 (any base^0 = 1)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state the loop invariant: what is accumulated in result after each iteration
Can explain why base^0 = 1 and how the loop handles it

common_mistakes.mistake: Initialises result to 0 instead of 1
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is the identity element for multiplication? If you start your accumulator at 0 and multiply, what will result always be?

exercises.title: GCD and LCM using Euclid's algorithm
exercises.concept_under_test: function with loop, Euclidean algorithm, call by value
function_prototypes: long long lcm(int a, int b);
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function int gcd(int a, int b) that returns the GCD of two positive integers using Euclid's algorithm (iterative, not recursive). Use it to compute and print LCM = (a*b)/gcd(a,b).
constraints: Use the iterative version of Euclid's algorithm
No recursion
Implement gcd() as a separate function
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace gcd(48, 18) step by step showing remainders
Can explain why LCM product requires long long for intermediate result

common_mistakes.mistake: Integer overflow in a*b for large inputs
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If a=100000 and b=100000, what is a*b? Can that fit in a regular int?

exercises.title: Call by value vs. call by reference — increment demonstration
exercises.concept_under_test: call by value, call by reference using pointers, difference in effect on caller
exercises.problem_statement: Write two functions: increment_by_value(int n) increments n inside the function but the caller's variable is unchanged. increment_by_reference(int *n) increments *n and the caller sees the change. Read an integer x, call both functions, and print x after each call to demonstrate the difference.
exercises.sample_output:
After call by value: 10
After call by reference: 11

test_cases.label: x=10
test_cases.expected_output:
After call by value: 10
After call by reference: 11

test_cases.label: x=0
test_cases.expected_output:
After call by value: 0
After call by reference: 1

constraints: increment_by_value must not use pointers
increment_by_reference must use a pointer parameter
Print x (not a return value) after each call
mental_model_checkpoints: Can draw a memory diagram showing why the by-value call does not modify x in main
Can explain what &x passes to increment_by_reference

common_mistakes.mistake: increment_by_value also modifies caller via return value — misses the point
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If increment_by_value returns n+1 and main does x = increment_by_value(x), have you demonstrated call by value or call by value with assignment? Are those the same?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
exercises.grading_notes: The printed output must show that x is unchanged after by-value and changed after by-reference.

SCOPE.name: Scope and Lifetime
SCOPE.description: ACP Unit 1 — Local vs. global scope, function prototype scope, static variables.

exercises.title: Demonstrate local vs global scope
exercises.concept_under_test: local variable, global variable, scope rules
exercises.problem_statement: Declare a global int counter. Write a function increment() that increments it by 1 and a function reset() that sets it to 0. Call them from main and print counter after each call to demonstrate scope.
constraints: counter must be a global variable
Do not pass counter as a parameter
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why counter retains its value between increment() calls
Can explain the difference between a local and a global variable's lifetime

common_mistakes.mistake: Declaring counter as local in main — increment() can't see it
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Where is counter visible? Can a function see a variable declared inside another function?

ARRAY.name: Arrays
ARRAY.description: ACP Unit 1 — Arrays, passing arrays to functions. Additional practice beyond the lab programs.

exercises.title: Reverse an array in-place
exercises.concept_under_test: array indexing, in-place swap, loop bounds
exercises.problem_statement: Read N integers into an array (N <= 100). Reverse the array in-place (no second array) and print it, space-separated on one line.
constraints: No second array
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state which two indices are swapped in the first iteration
Can state the correct loop termination condition (why N/2 and not N)

common_mistakes.mistake: Loop runs to N instead of N/2 — array reversed twice (back to original)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: For a 4-element array, how many swaps do you need? Trace your loop — how many times does it actually swap?

exercises.grading_notes: The N/2 loop bound is the primary teaching point.

exercises.title: Linear search in an array
exercises.concept_under_test: array traversal, linear search, return index vs -1
exercises.problem_statement: Read N integers into an array (N <= 100), then read a target value K. Print the index of the first occurrence of K, or -1 if not found.
constraints: Stop searching as soon as the target is found
Return/print -1 if not found
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why the loop must stop after the first match
Can state what the function returns if the array is exhausted without a match

common_mistakes.mistake: Continues searching after finding the target
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If you find K at index 2, should you keep looking at index 3? What does 'first occurrence' mean for your loop?

exercises.title: Bubble sort
exercises.concept_under_test: nested loops, comparison-based sorting, early termination
exercises.problem_statement: Read N integers (N <= 20). Sort them in ascending order using bubble sort and print the sorted array.
constraints: Implement bubble sort (not a library sort)
Add early termination when no swaps occur in a pass
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace one full pass of bubble sort on [5,3,1] showing each comparison and swap
Can explain why the inner loop bound decreases by 1 after each outer pass

common_mistakes.mistake: Inner loop bound does not shrink — does redundant comparisons on already-sorted tail
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After the first pass of bubble sort, what is guaranteed about the last element? Does it need to be compared again in the second pass?

exercises.grading_notes: Early termination flag required for full efficiency marks.

STRUCT.name: Structures
STRUCT.description: ACP Unit 2 — struct definition, typedef, member access, struct as function parameter.

exercises.title: Student record with struct — find top student
exercises.concept_under_test: struct definition, member access with dot operator, struct passed to function
exercises.problem_statement: Define a struct Student with fields: char name[50], int roll_no, float gpa. Read 3 students. Print the name and GPA of the student with the highest GPA.
constraints: Use an array of struct Student
Do not use dynamic memory
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state how to access the name field of students[1]
Can explain the difference between students[i].gpa and students->gpa

common_mistakes.mistake: Uses -> instead of . for non-pointer struct
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Is students[i] a pointer to a struct or a struct value? When do you use . vs ->?

ARRAYSTRUCT.name: Array of Structures
ARRAYSTRUCT.description: ACP Unit 2 — Passing arrays of structures to functions. Additional practice beyond the mandatory lab programs.

exercises.title: Employee salary records — average and highest paid
exercises.concept_under_test: array of structures, passing to functions, averaging over struct field
function_prototypes: void printSummary(int n, Employee emp[], float avg, int hi);
constraints: n must be read from user (not hardcoded)
Use separate functions for each operation
averageSalary() must return float, not print
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state how to access emp[i].salary inside a function
Can explain why averageSalary returns a float rather than printing directly

common_mistakes.mistake: Integer division when computing average — float truncated
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If total_salary is a float and n is int, what type does total_salary / n produce? How can you ensure float division?

exercises.title: Inventory management — search, sort, display
exercises.concept_under_test: array of structures, search by field, sort by field, modular design
function_prototypes: int searchByName(int n, Product products[], char name[]);
exercises.sample_output:
Found: Banana, Qty: 50, Price: 0.30
Sorted inventory:
Banana 50 0.30
Apple 100 0.50
Cherry 200 1.20

test_cases.label: found and sorted
test_cases.expected_output:
Found: Banana, Qty: 50, Price: 0.30
Sorted inventory:
Banana 50 0.30
Apple 100 0.50
Cherry 200 1.20

test_cases.label: not found
test_cases.expected_output:
Not found: Maize
Sorted inventory:
Wheat 20 40.00
Rice 10 50.00

constraints: Use strcmp() for name comparison
Sort must swap entire struct, not just one field
All operations in separate functions
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why swapping entire structs (temp = products[i]; products[i] = products[j]; ...) is needed for sort
Can state what strcmp returns for equal vs. different strings

common_mistakes.mistake: Sorts only one field (e.g., price) but leaves name and quantity in original positions
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After your sort swaps products[i].price and products[j].price, what is products[i].name? Is that consistent with the new price?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
exercises.grading_notes: Struct swap (whole struct, not field-by-field) is the key teaching point.

PTR.name: Pointers
PTR.description: ACP Unit 3 — Pointer declaration, address-of, dereference, pointer arithmetic. Additional practice.

exercises.title: Swap two integers using pointers
exercises.concept_under_test: pointer declaration, address-of operator, dereference operator
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function void swap(int *a, int *b) that swaps the values at the two addresses. Call it from main and verify with two user-supplied integers.
constraints: Must use a function, not inline swap in main
Temporary variable allowed
mental_model_checkpoints: Can draw a memory diagram showing a, b, and what *a and *b point to
Can state what &x gives when x is an int

common_mistakes.mistake: Swapping the pointers themselves instead of the values they point to
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After your swap function, if I print *a in main, will I see 7 or 3? Draw the memory layout and walk me through it.

exercises.grading_notes: Swapping pointers vs dereferenced values is the core teaching point.

exercises.title: Find maximum in array using pointer arithmetic
exercises.concept_under_test: pointer arithmetic, traversal without subscript operator
exercises.problem_statement: Read N integers into an array. Write a function int find_max(int *arr, int n) that finds the maximum using only pointer arithmetic (no arr[i] subscript notation inside the function).
constraints: No subscript notation inside find_max
arr[i] allowed in main for reading input
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what (arr + 2) evaluates to in terms of memory address
Can state what *(arr + 2) evaluates to in value

common_mistakes.mistake: Incrementing the original pointer — array base is lost
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If you do arr++ inside the function, can you still access the first element? What would arr point to after the loop?

exercises.title: String tokeniser using pointers
exercises.concept_under_test: char pointers, pointer-based string traversal, static local pointer, null terminator manipulation
exercises.problem_statement: Implement char* my_strtok(char *str, char delim) that returns a pointer to the start of the next token each time it is called. Demonstrate by splitting a CSV line 'apple,banana,cherry' into fields, printing each on a new line.
constraints: No strtok from string.h
May modify the input string (replace delimiters with null bytes)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace exactly what happens to the string in memory after the first call
Can explain why the function must remember its position between calls (static pointer)

common_mistakes.mistake: Not handling trailing delimiter or single-token string
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does your function return if the string is 'a,,b'? Trace through the characters one by one.

exercises.grading_notes: The static local pointer is the key concept. Students who use a global instead lose style marks.

PTRARR.name: Pointer Arithmetic and Arrays
PTRARR.description: ACP Unit 3 — Pointers and arrays, equivalence, pointer traversal.

exercises.title: Print array using pointer arithmetic only
exercises.concept_under_test: pointer increment, dereference, pointer-to-array equivalence
exercises.problem_statement: Read N integers. Print them using a pointer variable p that traverses the array — no arr[i] subscript allowed anywhere in the printing loop.
constraints: No subscript notation in the printing loop
Must use pointer increment (p++)
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what *p gives vs *(p+1)
Can draw the pointer advancing through the array in memory

common_mistakes.mistake: Uses arr[i] in the loop despite the constraint
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is arr[i] equivalent to in pointer notation? Can you write the same expression using * and +?

PTRF.name: Pointers and Functions
PTRF.description: ACP Units 1 & 3 — Passing by reference, output parameters via pointers.

exercises.title: Min and max by reference
exercises.concept_under_test: pass by reference, output parameters via pointers
function_prototypes: void find_min_max(int *arr, int n, int *min, int *max);
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function void find_min_max(int *arr, int n, int *min, int *max) that sets *min and *max to the minimum and maximum values in the array. Call it from main and print both.
constraints: Function must return void and use pointer output parameters
Do not use global variables
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why *min must be initialised to arr[0] (not 0 or INT_MAX)
Can trace what happens at main when find_min_max writes through the min/max pointers

common_mistakes.mistake: Initialises *min to 0 — fails for arrays of all-positive numbers
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If all values are between 10 and 100, and you initialise *min to 0, what will your function report as the minimum?

DYNMEM.name: Dynamic Memory Allocation
DYNMEM.description: ACP Unit 3 — malloc, calloc, realloc, free, dangling pointers, memory leaks.

exercises.title: Dynamic integer array with malloc — average
exercises.concept_under_test: malloc, free, NULL check, dynamic array access
exercises.problem_statement: Read N from the user (N <= 1000). Allocate an integer array of size N with malloc. Read N integers, compute and print their average (as a float to 2 decimal places). Free the array before exiting.
constraints: Must use malloc (not a fixed-size array)
Must check malloc return for NULL
Must call free before exit
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what malloc returns if allocation fails
Can explain what happens if free is never called (memory leak)

common_mistakes.mistake: No NULL check after malloc
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does malloc return if it cannot allocate the requested memory? What would happen if your program tried to write to that address?

common_mistakes.mistake: Forgets to call free
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After your program exits, does the OS reclaim that memory? What about a long-running program that allocates repeatedly?

exercises.grading_notes: NULL check and free are non-negotiable for full robustness marks.

exercises.title: calloc and realloc — growable integer buffer
exercises.concept_under_test: calloc, realloc, free, zero-initialisation, buffer growth
function_prototypes: int* grow_buffer(int *buf, int old_size, int new_size);
exercises.problem_statement:
Write a program that:
1. Allocates an int array of size 4 using calloc (verify all zeros).
2. Reads 4 integers into it.
3. Reallocates it to size 8 with realloc.
4. Reads 4 more integers into positions 4–7.
5. Prints all 8 elements.
6. Frees and sets to NULL.
exercises.sample_output:
Initial (calloc): 0 0 0 0
After first fill: 1 2 3 4
After realloc and second fill: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

test_cases.label: standard 8 ints
test_cases.expected_output:
Initial (calloc): 0 0 0 0
After first fill: 1 2 3 4
After realloc and second fill: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8

constraints: Use calloc (not malloc) for initial allocation — verify zero-initialisation
Use realloc (not malloc + memcpy) for growth
Check realloc return value for NULL before overwriting original pointer
Free and NULL the pointer at the end
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain the difference between malloc and calloc output (uninitialised vs zero)
Can state why realloc's return value must not immediately overwrite the original pointer (risk of losing memory on failure)
Can explain when realloc moves memory vs. extends in place

common_mistakes.mistake: ptr = realloc(ptr, ...) — if realloc fails and returns NULL, original memory is lost (leak)
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If realloc returns NULL because of out-of-memory, what happens to the memory that ptr was pointing to? How can you avoid losing that pointer?

exercises.grading_notes: Safe realloc pattern (temp pointer) is required for full robustness marks.

LINKEDLIST.name: Self-Referential Structures and Linked Lists
LINKEDLIST.description: ACP Unit 3 — Self-referential structures, singly linked list as an application of pointers.

exercises.title: Create and traverse a singly linked list
exercises.concept_under_test: self-referential struct, malloc for nodes, traversal with pointer, NULL termination
constraints: Every node must be allocated with malloc
append() must traverse to the end — no tail pointer
free_list() must free every node in order
No fixed-size arrays
mental_model_checkpoints: Can draw a box-and-arrow diagram for a 3-node list showing data and next pointers
Can trace append() for an empty list (head == NULL) vs. a non-empty list
Can explain why free_list must save next before freeing the current node

common_mistakes.mistake: free_list does: free(curr); curr = curr->next — accesses freed memory
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After you call free(curr), is curr->next still valid? What do you need to save before calling free?

common_mistakes.mistake: append traverses with head pointer — modifies head — list broken
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Inside append, if you do head = head->next to traverse, what does main's head variable point to after append returns?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
exercises.grading_notes: Save-next-before-free in free_list is mandatory. Robustness: empty list (n=0) should print 'NULL'.

exercises.title: Insert and delete nodes in a singly linked list
exercises.concept_under_test: linked list insertion at head/tail/position, node deletion, pointer relinking
function_prototypes: Node* insert_after(Node *head, int pos, int data);
exercises.problem_statement:
Using the Node typedef from the previous exercise, implement:
- insert_at_head: inserts a new node at the front, returns new head.
- insert_after(pos): inserts after the node at 1-based position pos.
- delete_node(data): removes the first node with the given data value; does nothing if not found.

Build a list [1,2,3], insert 0 at head, insert 99 after position 2, delete node with value 2. Print after each operation.
exercises.sample_output:
After insert at head: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> NULL
After insert after pos 2: 0 -> 1 -> 99 -> 2 -> 3 -> NULL
After delete 2: 0 -> 1 -> 99 -> 3 -> NULL

test_cases.label: full demo
test_cases.expected_output:
After insert at head: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> NULL
After insert after pos 2: 0 -> 1 -> 99 -> 2 -> 3 -> NULL
After delete 2: 0 -> 1 -> 99 -> 3 -> NULL

constraints: insert_at_head must return the new head
delete_node must correctly handle deleting the head node
delete_node must handle the case where data is not found
mental_model_checkpoints: Can draw the pointer relinking for insert_after at position 2
Can trace delete_node for the head node vs. a middle node vs. a node not in the list

common_mistakes.mistake: delete_node for head node: frees head but returns old head — main now has dangling pointer
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When you delete the head node, what must you return to main so it can update its head pointer?

common_mistakes.mistake: insert_after does not check for position beyond list length
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What happens in your code if pos is larger than the number of nodes?

style_requirements: Function comment block required for each function
exercises.grading_notes: Head deletion and not-found handling are the critical robustness cases.

STRING.name: String Handling
STRING.description: ACP Unit 4 — char arrays, null terminator, basic string operations.

exercises.title: Reverse a string in-place
exercises.concept_under_test: char array, string traversal, null terminator, strlen
exercises.problem_statement: Read a word (no spaces, <= 99 chars). Reverse it in-place and print.
constraints: Use a char array (not a string library reverse)
In-place reversal
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what strlen returns for 'hello'
Can identify the null terminator and explain why the swap loop stops before it

common_mistakes.mistake: Includes the null terminator in the swap loop
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What is stored at str[strlen(str)]? What happens if you swap that with the first character?

STROP.name: String Operations with User-Defined Functions
STROP.description: ACP Unit 4 — User-defined string functions and pointer-based string manipulation. Additional practice beyond the lab programs.

exercises.title: String length without strlen
exercises.concept_under_test: char pointer traversal, null terminator detection, user-defined string function
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function int my_strlen(const char *str) that returns the length of a string without using strlen(). Count characters until the null terminator. Read a string and print its length.
constraints: Do not use strlen() or any other string library function
Use pointer arithmetic (not array subscript) inside my_strlen
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state the null terminator's ASCII value and how to detect it
Can trace my_strlen for 'hi': which pointer values are visited?

common_mistakes.mistake: Counts the null terminator as part of the length
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does strlen('hello') return — 5 or 6? Does your function agree?

exercises.title: Count vowels and consonants in a string
exercises.concept_under_test: char pointer traversal, character classification, user-defined function
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function void count_vowels_consonants(const char *str, int *vowels, int *consonants) that counts vowels (a,e,i,o,u — both cases) and consonants (other alphabetic characters) in a string. Spaces and punctuation are ignored.
constraints: Handle both upper and lower case
Digits and punctuation are not counted as vowels or consonants
Use pointer output parameters for vowels and consonants counts
mental_model_checkpoints: Can list the vowel characters (both cases) that need to be checked
Can explain how to use isalpha() from <ctype.h> to exclude non-letters

common_mistakes.mistake: Counts space and punctuation as consonants
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Is a space character alphabetic? What does isalpha(' ') return? Should it be counted?

exercises.title: String copy using pointer arithmetic
exercises.concept_under_test: pointer-based string copy, null terminator, user-defined strcpy equivalent
exercises.problem_statement: Write a function void my_strcpy(char *dest, const char *src) that copies the string src to dest without using strcpy(). Use pointer arithmetic (not subscript). Read a source string, copy it, and print the copy.
constraints: Do not use strcpy() or memcpy()
Use pointer notation inside my_strcpy (not dest[i])
Null terminator must be explicitly copied
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace the pointer values of dest and src for 'hi' after each character copy
Can state why the null terminator must be explicitly written after the loop

common_mistakes.mistake: Loop copies characters but stops before null terminator — dest is not null-terminated
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: Your loop stops when *src == '\0'. Has that null terminator been written to *dest at that point?

FILE.name: File I/O
FILE.description: ACP Unit 5 — fopen, fclose, fprintf, fscanf, fwrite, fread, feof, ferror, file modes. Additional practice beyond the lab programs.

exercises.title: Write and read back integers from a text file
exercises.concept_under_test: fopen, fclose, fprintf, fscanf, FILE pointer
exercises.problem_statement: Read 5 integers from the user. Write them to a file 'numbers.txt' using fprintf. Then re-open the file, read them back with fscanf, and print their sum.
constraints: Must use FILE pointer
Must close the file before re-opening
Check fopen return value for NULL
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what fopen returns on failure
Can state why the file must be closed before reading back

common_mistakes.mistake: No NULL check on fopen — crashes if file can't be created
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What does fopen return if it cannot create the file? What happens if your program continues to use that value?

style_requirements: NULL check on fopen with appropriate error message

exercises.title: Read a file line by line using fgets with feof and ferror
exercises.concept_under_test: fgets, feof, ferror, error handling in file I/O
exercises.problem_statement: Write a program that reads a text file 'input.txt' line by line using fgets(), counts the total number of lines, and prints each line with its line number. Use feof() to detect end-of-file and ferror() to detect read errors. Create 'input.txt' by first writing 3 lines with fprintf.
exercises.sample_input:
Line one content
Line two content
Line three content
exercises.sample_output:
1: Line one content
2: Line two content
3: Line three content
Total lines: 3

test_cases.label: 3 lines
test_cases.input:
Line one content
Line two content
Line three content
test_cases.expected_output:
1: Line one content
2: Line two content
3: Line three content
Total lines: 3

constraints: Use fgets() for reading (not fscanf or fread)
Check ferror() after the read loop to detect I/O errors
Handle fopen failure with an error message
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain the difference between feof() returning true vs. fgets() returning NULL
Can state what fgets() stores when it reads a line — including the newline character

common_mistakes.mistake: Checks feof() before fgets() — classic off-by-one that reads last line twice
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When does feof() return true — before the failed read or after it? What does that mean for your loop structure?

common_mistakes.mistake: fgets() includes the newline in the buffer — prints extra blank lines
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What character does fgets() store as the second-to-last character for a typical line? How do you strip it before printing?

exercises.grading_notes: The feof-after-read pattern (not before) is mandatory for correctness.

RECUR.name: Recursion
RECUR.description: Recursive algorithms — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners who have completed all CO1–CO5 topics.

exercises.title: Recursive factorial
exercises.concept_under_test: recursion, base case, recursive case, call stack
exercises.problem_statement: Write a recursive function long long factorial(int n) that returns n! (0 <= n <= 20). Identify and implement the base case explicitly.
constraints: Must be recursive (no loop in factorial)
Must have an explicit base case with a comment
mental_model_checkpoints: Can trace the call stack for n=3: factorial(3) → factorial(2) → factorial(1) → factorial(0)
Can explain what happens without a base case

common_mistakes.mistake: Missing or wrong base case — infinite recursion
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: When does your function stop calling itself? What is the smallest input that goes straight to a return value without another recursive call?

style_requirements: Comment identifying base case and recursive case

UNION.name: Unions
UNION.description: Unions — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners.

exercises.title: Union memory sharing demonstration
exercises.concept_under_test: union definition, shared memory, sizeof union
exercises.problem_statement: Define a union Data with members: int i, float f, char str[20]. Print the size of the union. Store an int 65 in i, then read and print the value of str[0] (expect 'A'). Explain in a comment why this works.
exercises.sample_output:
Size of union: 20
As char: A

test_cases.label: standard
test_cases.expected_output:
Size of union: 20
As char: A

constraints: Must print sizeof the union
Must demonstrate value overlap via int and char member
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain why sizeof(union Data) == 20 (not 20 + 4 + 4)
Can explain why storing 65 in i makes str[0] == 'A' (ASCII)

common_mistakes.mistake: Confuses union with struct sizing
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: If a struct has an int and a char[20], what is sizeof(struct)? If a union has the same members, what is sizeof(union)? Why the difference?

ENUM.name: Enumerations
ENUM.description: Enumerations — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners.

exercises.title: Days of the week with enum
exercises.concept_under_test: enum definition, enum values, switch on enum
exercises.problem_statement: Define an enum Day (MON=1 through SUN=7). Read an integer 1-7. Use a switch to print the full day name. For invalid input, print 'Invalid day'.
constraints: Must use enum (not plain int constants)
Must use switch
mental_model_checkpoints: Can state what value MON has and what WED has
Can explain why enum values can be used in switch

common_mistakes.mistake: Forgets default case in switch
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: What happens in your switch if the user enters 9? What should happen?

FSEEK.name: Random File Access
FSEEK.description: fseek, ftell, rewind — not in the ACP syllabus. For advanced learners who want to explore random file access.

exercises.title: File size and reverse print using fseek and ftell
exercises.concept_under_test: fseek, ftell, SEEK_END, SEEK_SET, random access
exercises.problem_statement: Write a program that opens an existing text file 'sample.txt' (create it first with fprintf). Use fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END) and ftell(fp) to determine its size. Then use fseek to navigate to specific byte positions and print the file size in bytes.
constraints: Use fseek and ftell (not stat or platform APIs)
Check fopen for NULL
mental_model_checkpoints: Can explain what SEEK_END means as the origin for fseek
Can state what ftell returns — byte offset from start of file

common_mistakes.mistake: Does not rewind before reading content after seeking to end
common_mistakes.socratic_probe: After fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END), where is the file position? Can you read from that position?
